sections.heading: MMR 2 coverage rose nationally, but full-schedule completion remained much lower
sections.body: Among children aged 24 to 35 months, MMR 2 coverage increased from 66.4 percent in 2022 to 74.2 percent in 2025. Full vaccination according to the national schedule also rose, from 49.9 to 55.0 percent. The 2025 gap between the two measures was 19.2 percentage points, showing that MMR 2 receipt and full-schedule completion are not interchangeable. The inference is that many children reached the second measles-containing dose but still did not complete the broader national schedule.

sections.heading: The strongest regions on full-schedule completion were not always the strongest on MMR 2
sections.body: The National Capital Region had the highest 2025 full-schedule completion at 68.2 percent, followed by Davao Region at 66.7 percent, Central Luzon at 64.5 percent, and Ilocos Region at 64.4 percent. Ilocos Region, however, had the highest MMR 2 coverage by a wide margin at 95.6 percent. This means that a region can perform very strongly on MMR 2 while still leaving a substantial share of children short of full national-schedule completion.

sections.heading: Several regions showed wide MMR 2 to full-schedule gaps
sections.body: SOCCSKSARGEN had 69.0 percent MMR 2 coverage in 2025 but only 37.3 percent full-schedule completion, a gap of 31.7 percentage points. Ilocos Region had a similarly wide gap, with 95.6 percent for MMR 2 and 64.4 percent for full-schedule completion. Western Visayas and the Negros Island Region also had high MMR 2 levels, at 84.7 and 86.3 percent, but lower full-schedule completion, at 58.0 and 60.3 percent. These gaps point to schedule completion as a distinct challenge beyond delivering MMR 2.

sections.heading: BARMM remained the clearest low-coverage outlier
sections.body: BARMM had the lowest 2025 coverage on both measures, with 34.8 percent MMR 2 coverage and 14.3 percent full-schedule completion. MMR 2 improved from 26.0 percent in 2022, but full-schedule completion was unchanged at 14.3 percent. The gap between movement on MMR 2 and no movement on the full schedule suggests that progress on one vaccine marker did not translate into broader completion in the region.

sections.heading: Some regions moved backward from 2022 on one or both measures
sections.body: Cagayan Valley fell from 96.3 percent MMR 2 coverage in 2022 to 64.6 percent in 2025, while full-schedule completion dropped from 83.5 to 44.4 percent. The Cordillera Administrative Region also declined on both measures, from 76.7 to 59.1 percent for MMR 2 and from 59.3 to 40.3 percent for full-schedule completion. Zamboanga Peninsula¹ declined from 56.3 to 50.3 percent for MMR 2 and from 47.7 to 35.9 percent for full-schedule completion. These shifts show that the national increase masks substantial regional reversals.

sections.heading: Sex differences were small and moved in opposite directions by measure
sections.body: In 2025, female children had slightly higher MMR 2 coverage than male children, at 74.9 percent compared with 73.5 percent. Full-schedule completion was slightly higher among male children, at 55.7 percent compared with 54.2 percent among female children. The differences are modest, and the direction changes by measure, so sex is not a strong divider in this page's vaccination profile.

sections.heading: Higher birth order was linked to weaker full-schedule completion
sections.body: First-born children had the highest 2025 full-schedule completion at 63.4 percent, while children of birth order 6 or higher had the lowest at 41.8 percent. MMR 2 coverage varied less sharply, from 77.2 percent among first-born children to 70.0 percent among children of birth order 6 or higher. The wider full-schedule gap suggests that later-born children were especially less likely to complete the broader schedule, even when many still received MMR 2.

sections.heading: Vaccination card status was the sharpest divide in the data
sections.body: Children whose vaccination card was seen had 88.5 percent MMR 2 coverage and 74.0 percent full-schedule completion in 2025. Among children who never had a card, the corresponding levels were 31.0 and 14.4 percent. Children whose card was not seen or who no longer had a card were in the middle, at 61.0 percent for MMR 2 and 34.7 percent for full-schedule completion. This does not prove that the card itself caused vaccination, but it shows that documented contact with the vaccination system was strongly aligned with completion.

sections.heading: Urban children had higher MMR 2 coverage, while full-schedule completion was nearly even
sections.body: Urban children had 76.6 percent MMR 2 coverage in 2025, compared with 71.4 percent among rural children. Full-schedule completion was much closer, at 55.8 percent in urban areas and 54.2 percent in rural areas. The pattern suggests that the urban advantage was clearer for MMR 2 than for the full national schedule.

sections.heading: Mother's education showed uneven but meaningful differences
sections.body: Children whose mothers had Grades 7 to 10 education had the highest usable 2025 full-schedule completion at 59.9 percent, while those whose mothers had Grades 1 to 6 education had 46.7 percent. MMR 2 coverage was highest among children whose mothers had college education, at 77.6 percent, but their full-schedule completion was 53.9 percent. The no-education row was flagged in the display data, with 49.8 percent for MMR 2 and 33.5 percent for full-schedule completion. The pattern is not a simple ladder, but lower maternal schooling and flagged no-education values show weaker completion.

sections.heading: Wealth gaps persisted, especially between the lowest and better-off groups
sections.body: Children in the lowest wealth quintile had 66.6 percent MMR 2 coverage and 49.3 percent full-schedule completion in 2025. The highest quintile had the highest MMR 2 coverage at 81.9 percent, but full-schedule completion was 54.8 percent. The middle quintile had the highest full-schedule completion at 61.0 percent. Even though the pattern is not strictly linear across all quintiles, the lowest quintile remained clearly disadvantaged on both measures.

takeaways: National MMR 2 coverage rose to 74.2 percent in 2025, but full vaccination according to the national schedule reached only 55.0 percent.
The 19.2-point national gap between MMR 2 and full-schedule completion shows that receiving MMR 2 does not mean a child completed the broader schedule.
Regional patterns were uneven, with BARMM lowest on both measures and several regions showing wide gaps between MMR 2 and full-schedule completion.
Vaccination card status was the strongest divide, with much higher completion among children whose card was seen than among children who never had a card.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
